internal.name: Internal Self-Awareness
internal.description: Understanding your own values, passions, aspirations, fit with environment, reactions, and impact on others.
internal.indicators: You can articulate why you feel certain emotions, what you truly value vs. what you say you value, and how your behavior affects those around you.
internal.benefits: Greater confidence, stronger relationships, higher life satisfaction, better decision-making aligned with authentic values.

external.name: External Self-Awareness
external.description: Understanding how others view you in terms of your fit with the environment, reactions, and impact.
external.indicators: You accurately predict how others will react to your behavior, you seek and integrate feedback without defensiveness, and you adjust your approach based on others' needs.
external.benefits: Improved teamwork, better leadership, stronger interpersonal skills, reduced conflict.

comparisons.explanation: Tasha Eurich's research shows these two types are independent — you can be high in one and low in the other. Most people develop one more than the other. The most effective leaders and communicators develop both.

journaling.name: Journaling & Introspection
journaling.description: Writing about thoughts, feelings, and experiences to identify patterns.
journaling.best_for: Internal self-awareness, tracking emotional patterns, clarifying values.
journaling.limitations: Can become ruminative if overused; introspection without structure sometimes reduces accuracy.

feedback.name: Structured Feedback (360°)
feedback.description: Gathering input from colleagues, friends, and family using standardized questions.
feedback.best_for: External self-awareness, identifying blind spots, understanding impact on others.
feedback.limitations: Depends on honesty of respondents; can be affected by relationship dynamics.

assessment.name: Psychometric Assessments
assessment.description: Using validated tools like MBTI, Enneagram, DISC, or VIA Strengths.
assessment.best_for: Getting a structured framework for self-understanding, identifying personality patterns.
assessment.limitations: Self-report bias; should be interpreted by a trained facilitator; not deterministic.

comparisons.explanation: No single method is sufficient. Combine introspective methods (journaling) with external input (feedback, assessments) for the most accurate self-picture.

self_awareness.name: Self-Awareness
self_awareness.description: Objective observation of your internal states and their impact. Characterized by curiosity and acceptance.
self_awareness.approach: What am I feeling? Why? How does this affect my behavior?
self_awareness.outcome: Better decisions, emotional regulation, authentic relationships.

self_consciousness.name: Self-Consciousness
self_consciousness.description: Excessive focus on how you're being perceived, often with anxiety and self-criticism.
self_consciousness.approach: What do they think of me? Am I good enough? Everyone is judging me.
self_consciousness.outcome: Anxiety, avoidance, social inhibition, reduced performance.

comparisons.explanation: Self-awareness is non-judgmental observation; self-consciousness is judgmental evaluation. The goal is curious observation ('I notice I'm anxious') rather than anxious rumination ('I'm so anxious, something is wrong with me').

keyInsight: Self-awareness is a skill that can be developed through deliberate practice. The most effective approach combines internal reflection (journaling, mindfulness) with external feedback (360° assessments, trusted advisors). Avoid the trap of excessive self-consciousness — aim for curious, non-judgmental observation of your inner world.
